title: Red velvet cupcakes
subtitle: The American classic, cream-cheese-frosted
excerpt: Individual red velvet cakes: a cocoa-and-buttermilk sponge dyed deep crimson, topped with cream cheese frosting. The flavour is mild chocolate with a faint tang from the buttermilk. The colour is the occasion.
sourceNotes: Red velvet cake has documented American origins from the early twentieth century; the Waldorf-Astoria Hotel in New York is among the places credited with popularising the recipe in the 1940s. The crimson colour originally came from a chemical reaction between natural cocoa and acidic buttermilk; modern versions use food colouring to achieve it reliably. The recipe documented in American community cookbooks from the 1950s is in the public domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted cupcakes for up to 2 months. Frost after thawing at room temperature for 1 hour.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the cupcakes a day ahead and store in an airtight container. Frost on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven; fan at 160°C. Cupcakes bake quickly; check at 18 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The original red velvet colour came from anthocyanins in natural cocoa reacting with the acid in buttermilk, turning it a reddish-brown. Modern recipes use red food colouring to replicate the effect reliably regardless of cocoa brand.

content.text: is the reason for the buttermilk: acid keeps the crumb tender and gives the faint tang that makes the cream cheese frosting earn its place. The sponge is mild rather than intense chocolate — the cocoa is present but not dominant. Use a red food colouring gel rather than liquid; liquid colouring dilutes the batter and makes the colour muddy.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan) and line a 12-hole muffin tin with paper cases. Sift the flour, cocoa, bicarbonate of soda, baking powder, and salt into a bowl. In a separate large bowl, beat the softened butter and caster sugar together for 3–4 minutes until pale and fluffy. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Mix the buttermilk, red gel colouring, vinegar, and vanilla together, then add alternately with the flour mixture, beginning and ending with flour. Fold with a spatula — do not overbeat. The batter will be a vivid crimson.

Divide the batter evenly among the cases, filling each about two-thirds full. Bake for 18–22 minutes until the tops spring back when pressed gently and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack and cool completely before frosting.

Beat the cream cheese and softened butter together until smooth and lump-free. Add the sifted icing sugar and vanilla and beat until fluffy — 3 minutes on medium. The frosting should be thick enough to pipe. Use a palette knife for swirls or a piping bag fitted with a round or star tip. The
technique for swirling applies; pipe from the outside edge inward, building up to a peak.
